speaker: interviewee
text: How she was treated by her father all these years, it's a shame that my son is not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Showing scars from all the beatings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's see if we can finish this story in 20 minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any time, we can--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So that means we would--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll go to [NON-ENGLISH] now, to Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have-- yeah. We have to go to Liberation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the black guys, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] the running away in the death march. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hm? Yeah, I'll tell you in a second. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, and the DP camps, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So maybe we could get you-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] can you do it in 10, 12 minutes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to Liberation?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to skip that?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. To Liberation, quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Liberations? Up to the Liberations, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: In 10, and then from liberation-- it's going to be tough.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: But from Liberation--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm doing it, the whole. I told you I don't-- they don't give me enough time. I have to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Skip it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because we've got the wonderful stuff, the meat of this story, And in-- let's try and come to a stopping point, a closing point. You don't have to do much about your life here.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't tragically here, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think you can do from where we left to Liberation in 10 minutes and then the DP camps to the United States in 10 minutes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, you know I wasn't in a DP camp. I told you, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Traunstein, in the apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: In apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1 I think that's interesting that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Braun's parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva Braun's parents' apartment. So we could do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put 10 into 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you'll hit me on the head at 20?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if we can stop, we'll stop there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when we do the five pictures and then five--

speaker: interviewee
text: So shall I start now? What shall I start now?

speaker: interviewer
text: You should start now in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Goluchow and then--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is take five. Phyllis Dresen talking with Allen Caine in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. It's October 23, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once in awhile we could hear cannons, a lot cannons. That was a sign the enemy is getting close. The 19th of January, 1945, we packed up and we went into march. Halfway we marched, walked. We walked daytime, we walked nighttime, in the snow. It was very hard to do it. We lost a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is what would be called a death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's not the whole-- this wasn't the worst. And finally we came to one place. We took a train, took us into Berlin, Germany, outskirts of Berlin. Everybody got assigned to a barrack, and 150 people sat on the ground. There's no room for them, so they promised them, but there is an empty barrack-- let's invited-- they invited them into the barrack, the 150. They closed up tight, fumigated-- there's plenty of room now. We stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Oranienburg, Sachsenhausen, near Berlin-- suburb of Berlin. We stayed there a couple weeks. Cannons started getting louder. We were on our way again. This time it was less walking. We came into a place they called Flossenburg at Sudetenland. It was part of Czechoslovakia. All-- the whole time, there's all Germans. There was a death camp, like Birkenau. We didn't do any work because there was no work to do. I stayed there for awhile. One time I got beat up. They used to-- five people used to push a wagon, a flatbed wagon to pick up the deads. There's a lot of dead bodies laying all over the place, and they were getting close to me. I was laying on the ground, unconscious, and one guy knew me. He knew my brother. He ran and called my brother. He was in a different block, a different barrack. My brother came. It's all he had to do. Stood me up. They came with a push cart. They looked at me. They were just laughing. I wasn't still-- I was still unconscious, but I was on my feet, so they wouldn't touch you. In that place it was always foggy-- from burning the people. There was no crematoria-- they were burning them. There was a crack in the mountains and that's where they were throwing the people down, their bodies down. And they had a group of people who would burn them. These people would never see the shine. They never came up. They stayed there. When you looked down you couldn't see them. That's how deep it was. That's the place that separated me from my brother. That was a terrible thing for me. And I was assigned, again, for a march. It wasn't far. Put us in a train car and we were wind up in lower Bavaria, building a airfield for the airplanes. 12 o'clock every day, 12 o'clock the Americans used to come and bomb. There were a couple double mortars, airplanes. The American burned them. They used to go solo. They used to stick their heads out. They saw who-- who we are. Down there we hardly get any-- anything to eat. Water was unbelievable scarce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the last camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ganacker. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stayed in a-- in a woods. They dug us-- there are no beds. They dug a hole in the ground and for our heads to make a little step to lay our heads down, and only one blanket we got. People turned into cannibals. We could find bodies cut up. People were eating bodies. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The time came for the death march-- to march out of there. And by the way, something else I got to mention it. A-- a group of SS came down in civilian clothes. They checked me out. I didn't know what's all about. They undressed me, took a sample of my hair from this part, because here there wasn't enough hair to for it, from here. They asked me if I-- if anybody did any experiment on me-- they measured my chest, the color of my eyes, and I didn't know what's all about. As we left the place, a kapo-- a kapo comes over to me. He says you're lucky, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, why? What-- what would happen? He says, they were going to take you to a house of breeding children. Even though you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Exactly. I told you what they did. They took our-- the-- the Star of David away, only red. Red--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Hitler said no Jew will ever step over the threshold of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So not to make himself out a liar, so he took us and we just had one, the red Politsch. So nobody then didn't know that we are Jewish. And we were supposed to-- I was among them to breed children. There were a lot of Gentile people with us, too-- Jews, Jewish young kids. And then a year or two years, there you go, you were in Heaven. You don't live long. Well, anyway, we marched. We didn't have any water. I got so used to eat grass, just like an animal. My bowel movement was like a horse. I seen-- we had that one Algerian, an Arab, eating a live frog. We had Gypsies. They were talking in French to each other. He says, I'm getting too hungry. Sooner or later I'm going to start-- I'll kill somebody and I'll eat them up. I understood French. People were playing tricks, laying down, playing dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They found out that they playing dead, they had a special group. The SS, they used to go-- everybody who was laying on the ground, they put their nozzle to the apple, Adam's apple. They start shooting until the head disengaged from the body. All over the places were dead bodies, heads.

speaker: interviewer
text: In any of these camps, did you ever make contact with any partisans? Did they ever help?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were marching from Goluchow, on the way we had partisans. They didn't pay attention to us because we were Jews and we wouldn't got to show. A group of partisans came down, they could've opened-- in '44-- they could've opened the camp, likely without a shot. The SS would give up and free us. We were 96% Jews. They didn't do it. They went back to the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: They hated Jews more than the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the way, yes. They would like to see what happened to us. They would like to see what happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now here you are marching and you're-- the war is coming to an end.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war is coming to an end. As a matter of fact, we got-- we marched till the 28th of April. The 28th of April, Hitler committed suicide, and they didn't even know about it. And they didn't care. They were like leeches. Put their teeth in us, sucked our blood, and they wanted to take us to find out what to do with us. They had a mind to take us to Berchtesgaden. That's where Hitler's place was. We stopped the 28th of April. We stopped to take a rest and four of us-- I organized the four, an uncle of mine, and the other two were also from my hometown. And we disappeared into the woods. We walked, we walked, we walked. Finally, we came to a nice little-- what you see in the books-- a little building. I went in there, the first one. I spoke better English than-- I mean, better German than they did, my uncle and the rest of them. And I said to the lady, you give us a place here and you get an award for it. The war is to comes to an end. I didn't ask it-- I told her. She says, yes, don't come in my house, none of you. Because we were dirty, we were filthy. We didn't washed. We had lice, which they're afraid of that, the civilian Germans. And she gave us a shovel. She said, go, dig yourself a hole in the ground and hide. I found a place near the pigs. The pigs had a floor and we dug under the floor. And she used to bring us-- they had a little roof too. She used to bring us to eat. I didn't have to go to a toilet because we couldn't stand the food. It went right through us and we did right where we were sitting. We stayed there for about five days in that hole. Finally we found out that the war ended. We went to town. There was in the same building was a man. He was on typhus-- somebody left him there. And I said to my group, I said, let's go to town and get a doctor, a nurse, or anybody. Let him get that man to a hospital. He's one of our people. [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. As we came to town, the town's name is Eggenfelden, and I said, it's interesting. Look at that black people. I'd never seen a black person in my life. And we made a fire alongside them and little by little, they started coming over to us. And one black soldier comes over to me. My jacket was open. I had a little piece of beet, what they feed animals with it. I still had it-- it was already a couple weeks old. He puts his finger, he pulls it out. There was moldy already. I got mad. I was still afraid. I didn't trust the whole world. I may get hungry again. I grabbed his hand and I pulled it out and I put it back in, and I cursed him in German. Not that I-- I didn't howl it, but when he saw what I'm putting back, he-- he went over to a friend of his, puts his head down and he starts crying. It was a kid, about probably a 18-year-old boy. And there was a tank battalion. And one guy takes out banknotes, German banknotes. I threw it away right in his front of him. I said we want something to eat. And I had a suitcase with me, kept a empty suitcase just in case. So I filled it up, gave me Chiclets, cigarettes, the four cigarettes, ration cigarettes, pancakes. I filled it up. It was goodies in there, but nothing to put your teeth on it. I didn't know what chewing gum is, and I-- I sucked the sweetness off and then I spit it out. And then we went in. I still had-- I needed a pair of shoes. I was walking with wooden shoes. I went in a factory. I took out a couple dozen shoes on a stick. I saw my friends. I gave them away. I couldn't find a pair of shoes for me. My foot is-- had a big foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I have nothing to wear. There was a French army inside this city. I said to one Frenchman, where I can get a bakery? They have bread, and that's what I want.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- he showed me. I could speak French. I went into the bakery. They were just taking the bread out of the oven, had big line for blocks and blocks. Everybody moved aside. We went in. They kept the bread on the board. As it was the bread was cool enough, I bend over that bread and I picked up the board and I walked out with a whole loaf. It was about 10 loaves of bread. I walked right out. I said, my friends are hungry. They'll need something to eat. And I walked in the street. I see this guy, liberated. I know him. All of a sudden, I hear somebody calls me. [NON-ENGLISH]-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a Czechoslovakian. [NON-ENGLISH]. Oh, I heard that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother is alive, he said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I took off two loaves of bread and the rest I said come here, bend down. And I put the whole board on him. He said, here, the rest of the bread. I don't want no more as long as I got my brother, I don't want no more nothing. And that took about a week til I wrote him a letter to my brother through a German on a motorcycle, because I was too far to-- to get there. And the British were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote my brother a letter. If you're OK, I want to see you. And if I don't see you, you're not OK, I'm going to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was afraid that I can do that dirty trick-- go to Poland. And lucky, thank God, that I didn't go to Poland. Because Poland would have pogroms. They were killing left and right. Then my brother came, and he took me over to Traunstein. I stayed in a-- in a home--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what home did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Officer's home. And little by little, we gained weight, we gained a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an apartment there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Later on I had an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Traunstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: What apartment was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the apartment from Eva Braun's parents, Hitler's in-laws.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just plainly. I went up to the bergermeister, and I said, banging in the table, I live on the street and I want an apartment. You brought me here. You feed me, and you-- and I want to live here. He was afraid. And he gave me that apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about getting married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me tell you something. I had a girlfriend. I have it on one of the pictures. Did I mention that? She, you know, they were giving in the soup for girls-- our boys, they castrated them. The women, they did something that they wouldn't get their period. Some of the women turned yellow-- their skin was yellow. They dropped dead like-- like flies. And the girl that I met, she came to my hometown. She was still in her uniform, too. She had a number. And became friends. And she asked me if I can help with something. She needs clothing. And, yeah, I gave her clothing from top to bottom, underwear, everything. I knew a Nazi who had a store, and he hid all the stuff. I went over to him. I said, I'm a good guy. Let's live in peace. And I have a friend I want you to-- he had special for women's stuff. And I gave him bread, cigarettes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and whatever she wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what happened in Germany, I'll tell you another thing. Men that didn't get married, they lived together and they have children. Once they have children, then they got married. Because you couldn't ask a guy, are you castrated? Couldn't ask a girl, were you castrated? So they lived a year, two years. And if everything works out, she became pregnant. They wrote the rabbi already know these things. He didn't ask questions, why she's pregnant, even though they were Orthodox rabbis. They're from Russia-- they came from Russia, but most of them didn't survive, the rabbis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's how they-- and that's how they married. They lived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't want to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I just didn't want it. I just didn't want it. I didn't want to marry my kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to this country 1949, March the 3rd. That was the nicest, best day of my life. I came to civilization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who brought you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- President Truman gave out an order, 200,000 refugees. And the ones that were in with the numbers, they should be the first ones. So I was among the first ones to come to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next day, I went working my trade, cabinet making. I got a job with the Food Fair, if you remember. No, Food Fair wasn't in your parts. It was here. It's a big chain, a big chain, Jewish chain. See I had to have a sponsor, so they were my sponsor, at work. They-- I worked-- hardly worked with Jewish people and they--they liked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You learned English.

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned English. Took me a half a year. I was good in languages. I went out with a girl, spoke French with her, and then I forgot French.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in the same block. I think she told you that. My-- my-- may he rest in peace, my father-in-law noticed my number, and he was with his little boy at that time, and they were speaking Yiddish. So he came over to me and he started talking to me. Then we went to the same evening school together-- for-- for citizenship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went over to my brother one time. I said, you know what? I met a family here. It's unbelievable. There is a father and a mother, a daughter and a son, and a bubbe. I never saw that since 1939, I didn't see something like that. Since I-- I-- I'm sorry, since I came to camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: For three months, I didn't know there are Jewish women left alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And little by little-- so there weren't too many girls around, wasn't too many boys around for her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the reason I didn't marry in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '53-- '52, I think. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] February the 9th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, that's better.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've lived in Philadelphia all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no. We lived not just in Philadelphia. We lived in New Jersey, also. We did all kind of work in my life, had a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've been active in Holocaust groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I-- I just became active because all these years, sometimes I worked seven days a week. I worked three jobs in one time. I worked in a restaurant, I worked at a highway, and I did side jobs to support the family. I did pretty good [INAUDIBLE]. And later when the kids grow up, my wife helped me out. It was a big help. If a wife helps out, it buys a lot of groceries.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two. That's-- that's-- part of us. The best thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back to Germany to give testimony. Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was subpoenaed. My-- they didn't-- they just called me. They didn't call my wife. First, a DA came here, he interviewed me. And the guy from Goluchow, the commandant, he was on trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. . He was a half-breed-- half Polish and half German. And half a year or a year later, I got subpoenaed to go to Germany. And I didn't want to go by myself, so my wife writes a letter to them, if I can't go, he's not going. The next day, I got a ticket for her, also. They paid for my wages, for my everything they paid. And I went to Germany, Frankfurt, and [NON-ENGLISH]. Gave us a hotel. The next day I come in. As I stick my head in, I turned around. I'm running out. And he recognized-- well, he didn't recognize me. He figured that's me. They intrepreted-- I asked for interpreter. I didn't want to speak fluently myself English-- German. He begged me. Please, he said. I said, Germany doesn't have a Hall of Justice. Never had. He said, but this time we do. And he was-- we were just almost fighting together and finally I-- I went, sit down. They came in. They all greeted me. And they want me to look around, see if I see anybody. As I was talking to them, the commandant was sitting like that and listening, and I had a swivel chair. They wanted me to point him out. As I turned around, I pointed out like that. He thought I have a gun or something. He went like that. That happened to be on a holiday. That's why usually their kids used-- from school, German kids used to come around and listen to the whole thing. There were seven judges and one lady judge. They kept asking me a few times the same thing to-- to get me off the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What hat he was wearing. Did he wore a helmet. See, I used to shine his shoes. I used to clean his motorcycle. If the motorcycle had heavy tires or thin tires. OK, I gave them the answer. Another guy, he had two lawyers, asked me the same question. I said to the interpreter, I said, you tell them I'm not talking to them. I gave them the one answer and that's it. Then I broke down, I started to cry. He banged on that with that thing. He said, recess. We came back. The first thing they did, they stood up, apologized to me, and I told them, if you're going to ask me twice the same thing to confuse me, I'm not talking to you anymore-- because I knew the Germans. After the war they were scared, scared terrible. So-- and people from Israel came testify against him, from Argentina. We couldn't do a damn thing because we-- I testified that I saw him-- that he killed two Christian Hungarians, and he said he didn't. I couldn't testify that I seen it, which I didn't. I was locked up. I heard the shots. I saw him coming in with a machine gun, because he never had a machine gun. He only had a little revolver. And there wasn't enough. See, it's a democratic country. You had to see it to testify, and I didn't see it. I didn't want to lie to get a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the end was, years later, a friend of his-- he was also an SS man-- he saw him killing a guy, one of our people. His friend also, and SS man, that's when he got jailed. But when we testified, he didn't got jail. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then I-- What does that say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to-- he wanted-- he was walking around me, walking around me. I didn't want to talk to him. Nah, I'm the king-- not him. I didn't want to talk to him.
